ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (chicken sandwich) is the closest available match; no dedicated 'chicken burger' class exists in FoodOn as of 2024. The record describes a chicken patty served in a toasted bun with lettuce and tomato.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKN used as closest defensible match for a chicken patty in a bun. No exact FoodEx2 entry for 'chicken burger with lettuce and tomato' exists; close match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken burger as a composite food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard chicken burger (approximately 200 g) consisting of a chicken patty, toasted bun, lettuce, and tomato, as commonly served in Singapore fast-food and casual dining outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Burger (cr_id F05020023). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken at face value from this canonical source with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 21120 (fast-food chicken sandwich) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a chicken burger as a composite food. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the GI of a white-bread burger bun (GI ~70–72, University of Sydney GI Database) weighted against the protein and fat content of the chicken patty, which moderates the overall glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (66 × 41.06) / 100 ≈ 27.1. This is consistent with analogous fast-food sandwich entries in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 590 mg per 100 g; a standard 200 g serving delivers approximately 1180 mg sodium, representing more than half the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken burger consumption to occasional use, request no added sauces, and choose reduced-sodium preparations to keep single-meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (20.5 g/100 g) from the refined white bun with an estimated medium GI (~66); protein (11.6 g/100 g) and fat (13.3 g/100 g) partially blunt the postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should choose a wholegrain bun to lower GI, avoid sugary condiments, limit portion to one burger, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to moderate postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 13.3 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~3.2 g/100 g, combined with high sodium (590 mg/100 g) and potential trans fat from processed bun or frying oil, presents a composite cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit chicken burger intake, select grilled rather than fried patties, avoid processed cheese or mayonnaise additions, and monitor overall saturated fat and sodium intake across the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat from processed bun and potential frying oil is pro-inflammatory; sodium at 590 mg/100 g exceeds the RA sodium threshold and may worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chicken patties, verify bun ingredients for partially hydrogenated oils, and request reduced seasoning to minimise sodium-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A chicken burger is a Western-style sandwich consisting of a seasoned chicken patty — which may be grilled or deep-fried — served between a toasted white burger bun with fresh lettuce and sliced tomato. In Singapore, chicken burgers are widely available at fast-food chains, hawker stalls, and casual dining restaurants, and are commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or dinner. The texture contrasts a crisp or juicy chicken patty with a soft, slightly chewy bun and crisp salad vegetables. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 248 kcal, 11.6 g protein, 13.3 g fat, and 20.5 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 590 mg. The refined white bun contributes a medium estimated glycaemic index (~66), making portion control important for blood glucose management. The high sodium level makes this food unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis unless modified. The fat content, particularly if the patty is deep-fried, raises cardiovascular and inflammatory concerns. Choosing a wholegrain bun, a grilled patty, and avoiding high-sodium condiments significantly improves the nutritional profile. The dish is classified as halal and is gluten-containing due to the wheat bun.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken burger?
Is chicken burger high in sodium?
Is chicken burger suitable for hypertension?
Chicken burger vs beef burger calories
How much protein in a chicken burger?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020023) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fast-food chicken sandwich, item 21120) as an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white-bread bun GI data, which is the best available approved evidence for this composite food.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: HPB Foodo database entry F05020023 reports only energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre for this item. Detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrients, and moisture data are not available in the HPB record, and no Singapore-specific composite food analysis for this exact preparation was found in FAO/INFOODS SEA or ICMR-NIN as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 21120 (fast-food chicken sandwich) and should be verified against a direct HPB or laboratory analysis when available. Trans fat is set to -1 as no approved source reports a confirmed value for this specific item; the health_context notes for rheumatoid arthritis reference an estimated ~0.2 g/100 g from the input record's prior analysis, which is retained as a clinical caution only and not as a confirmed nutrient value. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on white burger bun GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for a chicken burger composite exists in the approved GI source list. Occasion tag 'breakfast' retained from input cultural metadata. Halal dietary flag retained from input.
